title: Trump's new tariffs on Brazil reflect the weakness of US trade strategy
gist: Trump's new 25% Section 301 tariff on Brazil, imposed after the Supreme Court struck down his IEEPA emergency tariffs, is riddled with exemptions covering two-thirds of Brazilian exports, exposing weak US leverage even as it accelerates Brazil's trade diversion toward China and India and hands Brazil a potent IP-based retaliation tool.

insights.claim: Two-thirds of Brazil's exports to the United States escape the new 25% Section 301 tariff through a combination of direct exemptions (44%), overlap with existing Section 232 tariffs on steel, aluminum, copper, autos, and lumber (13%), and special-provision carve-outs (9%).
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: The headline 25% tariff rate massively overstates real trade impact; investors modeling Brazil-exposed exporters or US import costs should use the effective ~33% coverage, not the sticker rate.
insights.quote: The exempted goods, together with those already covered by other tariff regimes, account for two of every three dollars' worth of goods the country sells to the United States.
insights.locator: The products subject to the new 25 percent duty

insights.claim: The exemption annex covers about 400 tariff subheadings including coffee (down to unflavored instant coffee), orange juice, beef, cocoa, nuts, tropical fruit, iron and manganese ore, chemical wood pulp, crude and refined petroleum, organic honey, and civil aircraft, with the aircraft carve-out reflecting Embraer's position as a top global builder of short- and medium-range commercial jets.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Embraer's commercial jet exports and Brazilian agri-commodity exporters (coffee, OJ, beef, cocoa) are effectively shielded from the new tariff wall.
insights.locator: The USTR's exemption annex covers some 400 tariff subheadings

insights.claim: The products actually left subject to the 25% duty are concentrated in a narrow band: machinery, electrical equipment, granite, gold, tires, sugar, and apparel.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Real tariff exposure narrows to a specific set of sectors, useful for pinpointing which Brazilian exporters or US importers actually face cost pressure.
insights.locator: The products subject to the new 25 percent duty

insights.claim: Pig iron was pulled off the tariff list after US foundries and steelmakers showed the USTR that over 95% of domestic pig iron production is consumed internally by integrated producers, leaving other US buyers dependent on imports, and that Brazil supplies more than half of US pig iron imports.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Shows the exemption process is driven by concentrated US industrial lobbying revealing real US import dependency, not policy generosity — a template for which other inputs get carved out next.
insights.locator: The exemptions list also includes pig iron

insights.claim: High-purity dissolving wood pulp was removed from the exemption list on a stated deforestation rationale, but the real driver appears to be that a US producer had just won a preliminary antidumping determination against Brazilian pulp producers in May 2026 — an inconsistent stance for an administration otherwise opposed to limits on US logging and drilling.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Signals that stated environmental or fairness rationales in US tariff actions are frequently pretexts for protecting specific domestic litigants — watch antidumping case outcomes as a better predictor of exemption status than stated policy rationale.
insights.quote: a strange argument from an administration opposed to limits on logging and drilling in US forests
insights.locator: high-purity dissolving wood pulp was removed from the exemption list

themes.theme: Legal fragility forces a slower, weaker instrument

insights.claim: The Supreme Court's February 2026 ruling that Trump's IEEPA emergency tariffs lacked a genuine emergency forced the administration to shift from executive emergency powers to the slower, more constrained Section 301 process involving notices, comments, and public hearings, which itself produced the extensive line-by-line carve-outs.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Future US tariff actions will likely follow this same statutory, hearing-based path rather than blanket executive orders, meaning more predictable but more exemption-riddled outcomes — useful for forecasting how aggressively new tariff threats will actually bite.
insights.quote: the process yielded to the reality that the US economy is integrated with other major global players such as Brazil
insights.locator: The Supreme Court's invalidation of the emergency tariffs

insights.claim: The 2025 tariffs of roughly 50% on Brazilian goods (including coffee) were struck down and their inflationary effect on US grocery prices forced this year's broader exemption list, now covering all forms of coffee down to instant and organic honey.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: US consumer price sensitivity, not diplomatic goodwill, is the real constraint on tariff scope — a leading indicator that food and grocery-linked imports will keep getting exempted from future tariff rounds.
insights.locator: The exemptions also recall the lessons from 2025

insights.claim: The temporary 10% Section 122 tariff that bridged the gap after the IEEPA tariffs were struck down lapses on July 24, 2026, pushing the administration toward the new Section 301 action as a more durable but procedurally slower replacement.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: July 24 is a concrete near-term date when the interim tariff regime expires and the new stacked tariffs take fuller effect — a catalyst date to watch for Brazil-exposed trades.
insights.locator: The administration had last year sought sweeping tariff protection

themes.theme: Stacked tariffs and a shaky fairness rationale

insights.claim: A separate Section 301 forced-labor investigation due July 24, 2026 proposes an additional 12.5% tariff on Brazilian goods, which stacked on the current 25% would rebuild a 37.5% trade barrier — approaching the 50% rate the Supreme Court struck down as unlawful in 2025.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Effective Brazil tariff exposure could nearly double again within days of publication, a concrete escalation risk for Brazil-linked supply chains and exporters to monitor around the July 24 decision.
insights.quote: Stacked, the tariffs would rebuild a 37.5 percent trade barrier, a number closer to the unlawful 50 percent imposed in 2025.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, a separate Section 301 investigation into forced-labor

insights.claim: The USTR's claim that it is 'fundamentally unfair' for Brazil to grant tariff preferences to Mexico and India under partial-scope agreements is undermined by the fact these arrangements comply with WTO rules that have permitted preferences among developing countries since 1979.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Exposes that at least one of the six counts underlying the forced-labor/unfair-trade case rests on a legally weak premise, raising the odds this component gets challenged or narrowed in negotiations.
insights.locator: Of the investigation's six counts, the least discussed

insights.claim: Negotiations to avert the Section 301 tariffs collapsed because US authorities demanded exclusive tariff concessions that Brazil cannot legally grant to one country unilaterally under its own trade law and its Mercosur/Latin American obligations.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: The negotiation impasse is structural, not merely political will — meaning a quick deal is unlikely unless the US drops its exclusivity demand or Brazil's Mercosur constraints change.
insights.locator: Negotiations to avert the Section 301 tariffs

themes.theme: The leverage the US claims to have doesn't exist

insights.claim: The United States actually runs a trade surplus with Brazil that more than doubled in 2025 to $14.4 billion, directly contradicting the premise that Brazil's trade practices are harming the US.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: A US trade surplus with the tariff target undercuts the stated justification for the action, suggesting the tariffs are driven by political signaling rather than an actual trade-deficit grievance.
insights.quote: The United States has a trade surplus with Brazil, which more than doubled in 2025, reaching $14.4 billion.
insights.locator: The fundamental inconsistency in the US approach

insights.claim: Since the first round of US tariffs in 2025, Brazil has posted record exports and diversified away from the US: China absorbed about 37% of Brazil's trade by end-2025, shipments to India rose more than 50%, and the US share of Brazil's trade is at a record low.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Tariff pressure is measurably accelerating Brazil's pivot toward China and India rather than forcing concessions — a durable trade-flow shift for investors tracking commodity and agricultural trade routes to reprice around, not a temporary dislocation.
insights.locator: Trade diversion around the globe as a reaction to US tariffs

themes.theme: Political backfire and Brazil's retaliation leverage

insights.claim: The new tariffs are likely to backfire politically inside Brazil by strengthening the domestic standing of President Lula da Silva, a figure Trump personally disdains — an outcome opposite to the administration's apparent intent.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Political risk analysts should weight Lula's re-election/approval prospects as a beneficiary of, not a casualty of, US tariff pressure.
insights.locator: Leaving aside the separate question of these tariffs backfiring politically

insights.claim: Brazil holds a potent retaliation tool in its Economic Reciprocity Law, which allows suspension of intellectual property obligations in agriculture (seeds), pharmaceuticals, and technology — sectors where US companies earn some of their most reliable income from Brazil — alongside an active WTO case against the United States.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: US seed/agbiotech, pharmaceutical, and technology companies with significant Brazilian revenue face a tangible IP-retaliation tail risk if the tariff dispute escalates further — a specific sector-level watch item.
insights.quote: The Economic Reciprocity Law's most potent instrument is the suspension of intellectual property obligations affecting sectors such as agriculture (seeds), pharmaceuticals, and technology—sectors where US companies earn their most reliable income from Brazil.
insights.locator: Brazil has also shown that it can retaliate

insights.claim: The author's central conclusion is that the tariff episode itself proves the weakness rather than the strength of US trade leverage over Brazil, arguing it is Washington, not Brasília, that is 'putting egos ahead of making deals.'
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Frames the broader US tariff strategy under Trump as structurally weaker than headline rates suggest — a contrarian anchor for assessing how much real pricing power the US retains in future tariff threats against other trade partners.
insights.quote: One can only conclude that those responsible for putting egos ahead of making deals are not the people sitting in Brasília, but those based in Washington.
